Predicting SEPTA Bus Stop Ridership

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

CPLN 5920 - Spring 2026

Author

Coco Zhou, Parker Otto, Veronica Zheng, Zhuosi Yang

1. Introduction

Philadelphia’s bus network plays an important role in connecting residents to jobs, schools, services, and other daily destinations. When the city or SEPTA considers adding a new bus stop, one important planning question is whether that location is likely to attract enough riders to justify the investment. Because ridership is shaped by more than the stop itself, this question requires looking at the surrounding neighborhood context, including who lives nearby, how people travel, what land uses are present, and how the proposed stop fits into the existing transit network.

This report develops a predictive model to estimate expected average daily ridership at potential bus stop locations in Philadelphia. The model uses demographic, land-use, and spatial characteristics around existing stops to understand which factors are associated with higher or lower weekday ridership. By learning from current stop patterns, the model can be used as a planning tool to compare candidate locations for future bus stops.

Data Sources: This analysis uses four types of data. The main dataset is the SEPTA Summer 2025 Bus Stop Summary from OpenDataPhilly, which gives average daily boardings and alightings at every bus stop collected by Automatic Passenger Counters. Demographic features come from the American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 5-Year Estimates at the Census tract level, accessed with the tidycensus R package. These include no-vehicle household share, poverty rate, population density, and transit commute share. Employment data come from the LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics (LODES) dataset produced by the U.S. Census Bureau. Total job counts are aggregated to the Census tract level and joined to bus stops to measure nearby employment activity. Spatial features come from several OpenDataPhilly datasets, including schools, hospitals, colleges, crash locations, business licenses, parcels, parks, and neighborhood boundaries. The city boundary is pulled with the tigris R package.

2. Data Preparation & Cleaning

Code 
library(MASS)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(tidycensus)
library(tigris)
library(janitor)
library(lubridate)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(gridExtra)
library(scales)
library(car)

httr::set_config(httr::config(ssl_verifypeer = FALSE))
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

2.1 Read Ridership Data

The primary dataset is the SEPTA Summer 2025 Bus Stop Summary, which provides average daily boardings and alightings at every bus stop, broken down by weekday, Saturday, and Sunday. Data are collected via Automatic Passenger Counters (APC).

Download: If you do not have the dataset, download it from SEPTA Ridership Statistics on OpenDataPhilly. Select the Bus Stop Summary file and save it to your local data/ folder as Summer_2025_Stop_Summary_(Bus).csv.

Code 
stop_ridership <- read_csv("data/Summer_2025_Stop_Summary_(Bus).csv") %>%
  clean_names()
Code 
head(stop_ridership, 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 17
         x      y   fid mode  source route direction stop_code stop    lat   lon
     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <dbl> <chr> <dbl> <dbl>
 1 -8.37e6 4.86e6     1 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25385 Ceci…  40.0 -75.2
 2 -8.37e6 4.86e6     2 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25386 Ceci…  40.0 -75.2
 3 -8.37e6 4.86e6     3 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25387 Ceci…  40.0 -75.2
 4 -8.37e6 4.86e6     4 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25388 Ceci…  40.0 -75.2
 5 -8.37e6 4.86e6     5 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25389 Ceci…  40.0 -75.2
 6 -8.37e6 4.86e6     6 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25390 Ceci…  40.0 -75.1
 7 -8.37e6 4.86e6     7 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25391 Ceci…  40.0 -75.1
 8 -8.37e6 4.86e6     8 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25393 Berk…  40.0 -75.1
 9 -8.36e6 4.86e6     9 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25394 Berk…  40.0 -75.1
10 -8.36e6 4.86e6    10 Bus   APC    3     Eastbound     25395 Berk…  40.0 -75.1
# ℹ 6 more variables: weekdays_o <dbl>, weekdays_1 <dbl>, saturdays <dbl>,
#   saturdays1 <dbl>, sundays_on <dbl>, sundays_of <dbl>
Code 
cat("Total stop-route observations:", nrow(stop_ridership), "\n")
Total stop-route observations: 18088
Code 
cat("Unique stop codes:", n_distinct(stop_ridership$stop_code), "\n")
Unique stop codes: 12927
Code 
cat("Unique routes:", n_distinct(stop_ridership$route), "\n")
Unique routes: 127
Code 
# Aggregate to one row per unique stop
# Some stops appear on multiple routes, so we sum ridership across routes per stop
stop_ridership_agg <- stop_ridership %>%
  group_by(stop_code, stop, lat, lon, x, y) %>%
  summarise(
    weekday_boardings   = sum(weekdays_o, na.rm = TRUE),
    weekday_alightings  = sum(weekdays_1, na.rm = TRUE),
    saturday_boardings  = sum(saturdays,  na.rm = TRUE),
    saturday_alightings = sum(saturdays1, na.rm = TRUE),
    sunday_boardings    = sum(sundays_on, na.rm = TRUE),
    sunday_alightings   = sum(sundays_of, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_routes            = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    weekday_total  = weekday_boardings  + weekday_alightings,
    saturday_total = saturday_boardings + saturday_alightings,
    sunday_total   = sunday_boardings   + sunday_alightings
  )

Because the raw ridership file is organized at the route-stop level, some physical stops appear more than once when they are served by multiple routes or directions. To create a stop-level ridership measure, we grouped the data by stop code, stop name, and coordinates, then summed boardings and alightings across route records for each stop. This produces one total weekday ridership value for each unique stop.

Code 
stop_ridership_agg %>%
  summarise(
    count = n(),
    min_weekday = min(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_weekday = median(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_weekday = mean(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_weekday = max(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
# A tibble: 1 × 5
  count min_weekday median_weekday mean_weekday max_weekday
  <int>       <dbl>          <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl>
1 12927           0             16         54.1        5911

From the table we can see that a few stops are very busy while many stops are not. This shows that our data is very uneven.

After aggregating to one row per unique stop, the dataset contains 12927 stops. SEPTA records ridership for three day types: weekday, Saturday, and Sunday. The chart below compares average total activity per stop across all three day types.

Code 
stop_ridership_agg %>%
  summarise(
    Weekday  = mean(weekday_total,  na.rm = TRUE),
    Saturday = mean(saturday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Sunday   = mean(sunday_total,   na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Day Type", values_to = "Avg Total") %>%
  mutate(`Day Type` = factor(`Day Type`, levels = c("Weekday", "Saturday", "Sunday"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = `Day Type`, y = `Avg Total`, fill = `Day Type`)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2C7BB6", "#ABD9E9", "#FDAE61")) +
  labs(title = "Average Total Activity per Stop by Day Type",
       x = NULL, y = "Avg Boardings + Alightings") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Weekday activity is much higher than Saturday and Sunday. Therefore, we will be using weekday total (boardings + alightings) as the outcome variable because it captures the most complete and policy-relevant demand signal for a typical workday.

2.2 Census / ACS Data

ACS 2023 5-year estimates are pulled at the Census tract level for Philadelphia County. These variables capture the demographic and socioeconomic characteristics most linked to transit demand.

API Key: A free Census API key is required. Register at api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html and set it once with census_api_key("YOUR_KEY_HERE", install = TRUE).

The following features were selected from the ACS:

  • Total population (B01003_001). Baseline count of how many people live near a stop.
  • Median household income (B19013_001). Income shapes car ownership and how much people depend on transit.
  • No-vehicle households (B08201_002 / B08201_001). Computed as a share. Car-free households are the most transit-dependent group.
  • Poverty rate (B17001_002 / B17001_001). Low-income residents are more likely to use transit for daily trips.
  • Renter share (B25003_003 / B25003_001). Renters tend to live in denser areas and rely on transit more.
  • Transit commute share (B08301_010 / B08301_001). The share of workers who already commute by public transit, which is a direct signal of existing demand.
  • Bachelor’s degree share (B15003_022 / B15003_001). Educational attainment used as a socioeconomic control.
  • Population density (derived from total population and tract area in sq miles). Denser areas tend to have more pedestrian activity and transit use.
  • Housing density (derived from housing units and tract area). A land-use measure of how built-up the area is around a stop.
Code 
# Reconstruct acs_tracts from precomputed stops_features.geojson + tigris tract geometries.
# This avoids an SSL/TLS handshake failure on api.census.gov (schannel on Windows).
stops_geo_tmp <- st_read("data/stops_features.geojson", quiet = TRUE)

tract_geoms <- tracts(state = "PA", county = "Philadelphia", cb = TRUE, year = 2023) %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

acs_vals <- stops_geo_tmp %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(geoid, no_vehicle_share, poverty_rate, renter_share,
         transit_share, bachelors_share, pop_density, housing_density,
         median_income) %>%
  distinct(geoid, .keep_all = TRUE)

acs_tracts <- tract_geoms %>%
  left_join(acs_vals, by = "geoid") %>%
  mutate(median_income_e = median_income)

rm(stops_geo_tmp, acs_vals)
Code 
# Variables already present from precomputed join — nothing to derive.

2.3 OpenDataPhilly Spatial Data

The following layers are pulled directly from OpenDataPhilly to construct spatial features around each bus stop. All sources are publicly available.

Schools. Philadelphia Schools (OpenDataPhilly) Proximity to schools captures student ridership demand.

Code 
schools <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/d46a7e59e2c246c891fbee778759717e_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Hospitals. Philadelphia Hospitals (OpenDataPhilly) Major medical facilities are consistent high-volume trip generators.

Code 
hospitals <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/df8dc18412494e5abbb021e2f33057b2_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Colleges & Universities. Philadelphia Colleges and Universities (OpenDataPhilly) College campuses generate significant transit ridership, especially for car-free students.

Code 
colleges <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/8ad76bc179cf44bd9b1c23d6f66f57d1_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Pedestrian & Bike Crashes. Crashes (OpenDataPhilly) Crash density serves as a proxy for pedestrian street activity near stops.

Code 
crashes <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/21154a8f1894449f839d2288726e9837_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Street Intersection. Intersections Control (OpenDataPhilly) Intersection density captures street connectivity and pedestrian walkability around a stop.

Code 
intersections <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/cc0e1bfe7a3d4814a0a0329c2650c62e_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Business Licenses. Business Licenses (OpenDataPhilly) Active business licenses are used as a proxy for commercial activity and foot traffic near stops.

Code 
businesses <- st_read(
  "https://phl.carto.com/api/v2/sql?q=SELECT+*+FROM+business_licenses&filename=business_licenses&format=geojson&skipfields=cartodb_id",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Property Parcels. Philadelphia Land Use (OpenDataPhilly) Parcel data are used to count commercial, residential, and vacant land uses within a buffer.

Code 
parcels <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/e433504739bd41049de5d8f4a22d34ba_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Parks & Recreation. PPR Properties (OpenDataPhilly) Parks and recreation sites generate leisure-trip ridership.

Code 
parks <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/d52445160ab14380a673e5849203eb64_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Neighborhood Boundaries. Philadelphia Neighborhoods (OpenDataPhilly) Neighborhood identifiers are used to add fixed effects that control for unmeasured place-based variation.

Code 
neighborhoods <- st_read(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/opendataphilly/open-geo-data/master/philadelphia-neighborhoods/philadelphia-neighborhoods.geojson",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

Employment (LODES/LEHD). LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics (Census Bureau) Job counts at the Census tract level are used as a measure of nearby employment activity around each stop.

Code 
lodes_url <- "https://lehd.ces.census.gov/data/lodes/LODES8/pa/wac/pa_wac_S000_JT00_2021.csv.gz"

lodes_tract <- read_csv(lodes_url) %>%
  mutate(tract_geoid = str_sub(as.character(w_geocode), 1, 11)) %>%
  group_by(tract_geoid) %>%
  summarise(total_jobs = sum(C000, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

All Transit Stops (Bus, Trolley, Rail). SEPTA Routes, Stops, and Locations (OpenDataPhilly) Rail and trolley stop locations are used to measure distance from each bus stop to the nearest higher-order transit service.

Code 
transit_stops <- st_read(
  "https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/b227f3ddbe3e47b4bcc7b7c65ef2cef6_0/downloads/data?format=geojson&spatialRefId=4326&where=1%3D1",
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272) %>%
  filter(grepl("^[A-Za-z]", LineAbbr))

Philadelphia City Boundary. Pulled via the tigris package. No API key required.

Code 
philly <- counties(state = "PA", cb = TRUE) %>%
  filter(NAME == "Philadelphia") %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272)

2.4 Data Cleaning

The raw ridership file has one row per stop per route, so each physical stop appears multiple times. After grouping to one row per stop, we apply two filters before modeling: we drop stops with no geographic coordinates, and stops that fall outside the Philadelphia city boundary.

Code 
# Step 1: baseline, unique stops after aggregation
n_raw <- nrow(stop_ridership_agg)

# Step 2: remove missing coordinates
stops_no_na <- stop_ridership_agg %>%
  filter(!is.na(lat), !is.na(lon))
n_after_coords <- nrow(stops_no_na)

# Step 3: convert to sf and clip to Philadelphia boundary
# Pennsylvania State Plane South (feet) for distance calculations
stops_sf <- stops_no_na %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 2272) %>% 
  select(-x, -y) %>%
  st_filter(philly)
n_after_philly <- nrow(stops_sf)
Code 
tibble(
  Step = c(
    "Aggregate stop-route rows to unique stops",
    "Remove stops with missing coordinates",
    "Clip to Philadelphia city boundary",
    "Final analytic sample"
  ),
  Rows = c(
    n_raw,
    n_after_coords,
    n_after_philly,
    n_after_philly
  ),
  Removed = c(
    0L,
    n_raw - n_after_coords,
    n_after_coords - n_after_philly,
    0L
  )
) %>%
  kable(
    col.names = c("Step", "Rows", "Removed"),
    caption = "Data Cleaning Log: SEPTA Bus Stop Ridership",
    format.args = list(big.mark = ",")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  ) %>%
  row_spec(4, bold = TRUE)
Data Cleaning Log: SEPTA Bus Stop Ridership
Step Rows Removed
Aggregate stop-route rows to unique stops 12,927 0
Remove stops with missing coordinates 12,927 0
Clip to Philadelphia city boundary 7,588 5,339
Final analytic sample 7,588 0

After aggregating route-stop records into unique stop locations, the dataset contained 12,927 stops. No stops were removed for missing coordinates, indicating that all records had valid spatial information for mapping and spatial joins. The main reduction occurred when the dataset was clipped to the Philadelphia city boundary, which removed 5,339 stops outside the study area. The final analytic sample contains 7,588 Philadelphia bus stops. Zero-ridership stops were retained because the outcome is a count variable and Poisson models can accommodate zero values, allowing the model to capture the full range of weekday ridership conditions rather than only stops with existing passenger activity.

Data Cleaning Decisions:

  • Missing coordinates (0 stops removed). Stops that have no latitude or longitude cannot be placed on the map or linked to neighborhood features, so they are dropped.
  • Outside Philadelphia (5339 stops removed). SEPTA routes extend into surrounding counties, but this project focuses on Philadelphia. Stops outside the city boundary would have no matching neighborhood or Census features, so they are excluded.
  • The 7588 stops in stops_sf make up the final sample used for all mapping, feature building, and modeling.

3. Exploratory Data Analysis

3.1 Distribution of Weekday Total

Code 
ggplot(stop_ridership_agg, aes(x = weekday_total)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#2C7BB6", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  labs(title = "Weekday Boardings per Stop (Raw)",
       x = "Weekday Boardings", y = "Number of Stops") +
  theme_minimal()

The raw distribution is heavily right-skewed. Most stops have very few boardings, but a small number of stops record very high ridership. This kind of skew is common for count outcomes.

3.2 Spatial Distribution of Bus Stops

Code 
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly, fill = "#f0f0f0", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_sf(
    data = stops_sf,
    aes(color = weekday_total),
    size = 0.6,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(
    option = "magma",
    direction = -1,
    name = "Weekday\nBoardings",
    trans = "log1p",
    breaks = c(0, 100, 500, 1000, 2500, 5000),
    labels = scales::comma
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "SEPTA Bus Stop Ridership",
    subtitle = "Summer 2025: Weekday Boardings per Stop"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14)
  )

Stops with high ridership are clustered along major corridors in Center City, North Philadelphia, and West Philadelphia, where transit infrastructure is dense and many residents rely on transit. Stops in Northeast and Southwest Philadelphia tend to have much lower ridership.

3.3 Ridership by Neighborhood

Code 
stops_with_hood <- stops_sf %>%
  st_join(neighborhoods %>% select(MAPNAME), join = st_within) %>%
  st_drop_geometry()

hood_ridership <- stops_with_hood %>%
  group_by(MAPNAME) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_boardings   = round(mean(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    median_boardings = median(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_stops         = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_boardings))
Code 
hood_ridership %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  kable(
    col.names = c("Neighborhood", "Avg Boardings", "Median Boardings", "Number of Stops"),
    caption = "Top 10 Neighborhoods by Average Weekday Boardings"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Top 10 Neighborhoods by Average Weekday Boardings
Neighborhood Avg Boardings Median Boardings Number of Stops
Center City East 363.7 263.0 18
Wissahickon Park 277.8 10.0 15
Washington Square West 275.8 119.0 69
Rittenhouse 261.7 118.5 98
Chinatown 252.5 242.0 4
Old City 247.1 160.5 66
Frankford 226.6 52.0 125
Wister 213.0 117.0 10
University City 211.7 125.0 101
Fern Rock 181.3 42.0 35

Top 10 Neighborhoods by Average Weekday Boardings

Code 
neighborhoods %>%
  left_join(hood_ridership, by = "MAPNAME") %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = avg_boardings), color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "magma", direction = -1,
    name = "Avg Weekday\nBoardings",
    na.value = "grey85",
    labels = scales::comma
  ) +
  labs(title = "Average Weekday Boardings by Neighborhood",
       subtitle = "Summer 2025") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))

Darker purple areas represent neighborhoods where the average bus stop has higher weekday boardings, while lighter yellow areas represent lower average stop activity. The map shows that weekday ridership is spatially uneven across the city, with higher average boardings concentrated in selected central and transit-oriented neighborhoods, including parts of Center City, South Philadelphia, West Philadelphia, and North Philadelphia. Lower average boardings appear more common in outer neighborhoods, particularly in portions of the Northeast and other lower-density edge areas.

3.4 Demographic Correlates

Because weekday boardings are heavily right-skewed, plotting them on the raw scale would compress most stops onto a flat band at the bottom and obscure any relationship with the demographic variables. To make the patterns readable, the y-axis in the scatter plots below is shown on a log scale (log(weekday_total)).

Code 
stops_census <- stops_sf %>%
  st_join(
    acs_tracts %>% 
      select(
        no_vehicle_share, poverty_rate, pop_density,
        transit_share, median_income_e, renter_share
      ),
    join = st_within
  ) %>%
  st_drop_geometry()
Code 
vars_labels <- c(
  no_vehicle_share = "No-Vehicle HH Share",
  poverty_rate     = "Poverty Rate",
  pop_density      = "Population Density (per sq mi)",
  transit_share    = "Transit Commute Share"
)

stops_census %>%
  dplyr::select(
    weekday_total,
    no_vehicle_share,
    poverty_rate,
    pop_density,
    transit_share
  ) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = -weekday_total,
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    variable = dplyr::recode(variable, !!!vars_labels)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = value, y = log(weekday_total))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.8, color = "#2C7BB6") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D7191C", se = TRUE, linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_x", ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Log Weekday Boardings vs. Demographic Variables",
    x = NULL,
    y = "log(Weekday Boardings)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

Stops in tracts with higher no-vehicle household shares, higher poverty rates, and higher transit commute shares tend to have more weekday boardings. This matches the idea that transit-dependent residents make more trips by bus. Population density also shows a positive relationship, as denser areas tend to support more transit use. The wide scatter across all four plots suggests that no single variable explains ridership on its own, which points to the need for a multi-variable model.

4. Feature Engineering

Building on the EDA, we now construct the predictor set for modeling. Features fall into five groups: Census tract attributes (demographics + jobs), neighborhood identifiers for fixed effects, distances to the nearest amenities, density counts within a 1/2-mile pedestrian catchment, and route-level service characteristics. The end product is a single stops_features table with one row per bus stop, ready for Section 5.

All spatial operations are performed in EPSG:2272 (Pennsylvania State Plane South, feet), which is the CRS used throughout Section 2. Distances and buffer widths are therefore in feet with no unit conversion.

4.1 Census Tract and Employment Attributes

We attach each stop to its surrounding Census tract with a point-in-polygon spatial join (st_within). This brings in the nine ACS variables built in Section 2.2 (no-vehicle share, poverty rate, renter share, transit commute share, bachelor’s share, population density, housing density, and median income) together with the LODES tract-level job count. A small number of stops at the city edge fall just outside every tract polygon due to coordinate precision; these are assigned to the nearest tract with st_nearest_feature() rather than dropped.

Code 
acs_for_join <- acs_tracts %>%
  select(geoid, no_vehicle_share, poverty_rate, renter_share,
         transit_share, bachelors_share, pop_density, housing_density,
         median_income = median_income_e)

stops_acs <- stops_sf %>%
  st_join(acs_for_join, join = st_nearest_feature) %>%
  left_join(lodes_tract, by = c("geoid" = "tract_geoid")) %>%
  mutate(total_jobs = replace_na(total_jobs, 0))

Every stop now carries the nine ACS features plus `total_jobs` from LODES.

4.2 Neighborhood Identifiers

We assign each bus stop to a Philadelphia neighborhood using a point-in-polygon spatial join, which identifies which neighborhood boundary each stop falls within. The neighborhood name is later used in Section 5 as a categorical fixed effect. This helps control for neighborhood-level differences that are not fully captured by the ACS variables, such as historic transit investment, local land-use patterns, or other place-based travel behaviors. For the small number of stops that do not fall inside any neighborhood polygon, we assign them to the nearest neighborhood so they can remain in the analysis.

Code 
stops_hood <- stops_acs %>%
  st_join(neighborhoods %>% select(MAPNAME), join = st_within)

na_idx <- which(is.na(stops_hood$MAPNAME))
if (length(na_idx) > 0) {
  nearest_hood <- st_nearest_feature(stops_sf[na_idx, ], neighborhoods)
  stops_hood$MAPNAME[na_idx] <- neighborhoods$MAPNAME[nearest_hood]
}

stops_hood <- stops_hood %>%
  rename(neighborhood = MAPNAME) %>%
  mutate(neighborhood = factor(neighborhood))

n_neighborhoods <- nlevels(stops_hood$neighborhood)

Stops are distributed across 157 neighborhoods, which is the number of fixed effects the model will estimate.

4.3 Distance to Nearest Amenity

For each stop we compute the Euclidean distance to the nearest instance of five amenity types using st_nearest_feature() paired with st_distance(). Each raw distance is also log-transformed with log1p, because transit demand typically decays multiplicatively with distance rather than linearly.

  • Schools. Student ridership demand.
  • Hospitals. Consistent high-volume trip generators.
  • Colleges and universities. Dense student-commuter activity.
  • Parks. Leisure-trip generators.
  • Rail and trolley stops. Proximity to higher-order transit, a proxy for bus-to-rail transfer demand.
Code 
nearest_dist <- function(points, target) {
  idx <- st_nearest_feature(points, target)
  as.numeric(st_distance(points, target[idx, ], by_element = TRUE))
}

d_school   <- nearest_dist(stops_hood, schools)
d_hospital <- nearest_dist(stops_hood, hospitals)
d_college  <- nearest_dist(stops_hood, colleges)
d_park     <- nearest_dist(stops_hood, parks)
d_rail     <- nearest_dist(stops_hood, transit_stops)

stops_dist <- stops_hood %>%
  mutate(
    dist_school       = d_school,
    dist_hospital     = d_hospital,
    dist_college      = d_college,
    dist_park         = d_park,
    dist_rail         = d_rail,
    log_dist_school   = log1p(d_school),
    log_dist_hospital = log1p(d_hospital),
    log_dist_college  = log1p(d_college),
    log_dist_park     = log1p(d_park),
    log_dist_rail     = log1p(d_rail)
  )
Code 
stops_dist %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(dist_school, dist_hospital, dist_college, dist_park, dist_rail) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   list(Median = median, Mean = mean, Max = max),
                   .names = "{.col}__{.fn}")) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = c("Variable", "Stat"),
               names_sep = "__") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Stat, values_from = value) %>%
  mutate(across(c(Median, Mean, Max), ~ round(.x, 0))) %>%
  kable(caption = "Summary of Distance-to-Amenity Features (feet)",
        format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Summary of Distance-to-Amenity Features (feet)
Variable Median Mean Max
dist_school 1,077 1,282 11,313
dist_hospital 4,464 5,419 27,152
dist_college 3,678 5,213 26,519
dist_park 791 963 8,846
dist_rail 1,869 2,892 16,207

The median stop is within a few thousand feet of the nearest school and park. Distances to colleges and hospitals show much wider spread because those amenities are fewer and more geographically concentrated, which is exactly why log-transforming is appropriate before modeling.

4.4 Density Counts within a 1/2-Mile Pedestrian Walkshed

A 1/2-mile buffer (2,640 ft) corresponds to roughly a 10-minute walk at average pedestrian speed and is the standard catchment radius used in transit planning for stop-area demand analysis. Within each stop’s buffer, we then count the features that drive trip generation and walkability.

Counts are produced with lengths(st_intersects()), which is vectorized and processes all stops in a single pass per layer. For the parcel layer (several hundred thousand polygons), we convert parcels to representative points with st_point_on_surface() before counting to keep the intersection fast. Parcels are classified by the first digit of the Philadelphia land-use code (c_dig1): 1 = residential, 2 = commercial, 9 = vacant. Other codes (industrial, institutional, transportation, recreation, water) are grouped into “other” and not counted separately, because they either overlap with layers already captured (parks, schools) or contribute little to boarding demand.

For the “other stops within buffer” feature, each stop’s own point necessarily falls inside its own buffer, so we subtract one from the raw count.

Code 
parcels_lu <- parcels %>%
  mutate(lu_class = case_when(
    as.character(c_dig1) == "1" ~ "residential",
    as.character(c_dig1) == "2" ~ "commercial",
    as.character(c_dig1) == "9" ~ "vacant",
    TRUE ~ "other"
  )) %>%
  st_point_on_surface()

parcels_res <- parcels_lu %>% filter(lu_class == "residential")
parcels_com <- parcels_lu %>% filter(lu_class == "commercial")
parcels_vac <- parcels_lu %>% filter(lu_class == "vacant")
Code 
buffer_ft <- 2640  # 1/2 mile ≈ 10-minute walk

stop_buffers <- st_buffer(stops_dist, buffer_ft)

count_in_buffer <- function(buffers, target) {
  lengths(st_intersects(buffers, target))
}

stops_buf <- stops_dist %>%
  mutate(
    n_schools_halfmi       = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, schools),
    n_hospitals_halfmi     = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, hospitals),
    n_parks_halfmi         = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, parks),
    n_businesses_halfmi    = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, businesses),
    n_intersections_halfmi = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, intersections),
    n_crashes_halfmi       = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, crashes),
    n_other_stops_halfmi   = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, stops_dist) - 1L,
    n_residential_halfmi   = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, parcels_res),
    n_commercial_halfmi    = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, parcels_com),
    n_vacant_halfmi        = count_in_buffer(stop_buffers, parcels_vac)
  )
Code 
stops_buf %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(ends_with("_halfmi")) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   list(Median = median, Mean = mean, Max = max),
                   .names = "{.col}__{.fn}")) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = c("Variable", "Stat"),
               names_sep = "__") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Stat, values_from = value) %>%
  mutate(across(c(Median, Mean, Max), ~ round(.x, 1))) %>%
  kable(caption = "Summary of 1/2-Mile Buffer Counts per Stop",
        format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Summary of 1/2-Mile Buffer Counts per Stop
Variable Median Mean Max
n_schools_halfmi 4 4.3 20
n_hospitals_halfmi 0 0.4 4
n_parks_halfmi 4 5.2 27
n_businesses_halfmi 3,453 3,669.5 12,660
n_intersections_halfmi 137 139.0 301
n_crashes_halfmi 267 289.7 901
n_other_stops_halfmi 59 63.5 169
n_residential_halfmi 3,600 3,877.4 13,150
n_commercial_halfmi 168 221.6 1,013
n_vacant_halfmi 152 427.8 3,828

The buffer summary shows that business licenses, residential parcels, and intersections have the largest raw counts within a half-mile of most stops, which is expected in a dense urban setting like Philadelphia. Residential parcels are especially common, with a median of 3,600 parcels per buffer, compared with 168 commercial parcels and 152 vacant parcels. This suggests that most bus stops are surrounded by housing-heavy land uses, even when commercial activity is also nearby. Crash counts are also relatively high, with a median of 267 crashes per buffer, but these should be interpreted as indicators of overall street activity and exposure rather than only unsafe driving behavior. In general, places with more people walking, driving, and transferring between modes are more likely to record higher crash counts.

4.5 Route and Service Features

Two remaining features describe the stop itself rather than its surroundings.

  • n_routes: the number of SEPTA bus routes serving the stop, carried over from the aggregation step in Section 2.1. Stops served by multiple routes function as transfer points and typically attract more boardings.
  • has_rail_access: a binary indicator for whether a rail or trolley stop lies within the same 1/2-mile buffer (i.e., dist_rail ≤ 2,640 ft). Rail access enables bus-to-rail chained trips and raises the effective service quality of the stop.
Code 
stops_features <- stops_buf %>%
  mutate(has_rail_access = as.integer(dist_rail <= buffer_ft)) %>%
  # drop ridership columns not used as predictors
  select(-c(weekday_alightings, saturday_boardings, saturday_alightings,
            sunday_boardings, sunday_alightings)) # Removed x, y here as I got rid of them earlier
Code 
stops_features %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  summarise(
    `Stops (n)`              = n(),
    `Mean routes per stop`   = round(mean(n_routes, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    `Max routes per stop`    = max(n_routes, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Stops with rail access` = sum(has_rail_access),
    `Share with rail access` = paste0(round(mean(has_rail_access) * 100, 1), "%")
  ) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.character)) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") %>%
  kable(caption = "Summary of Service Features") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Summary of Service Features
Metric Value
Stops (n) 7588
Mean routes per stop 1.42
Max routes per stop 12
Stops with rail access 4670
Share with rail access 61.5%

The resulting table stops_features has one row per bus stop and combines the outcome (weekday_total) with the tract attributes from 4.1, the neighborhood identifier from 4.2, the ten distance variables from 4.3, the ten buffer counts from 4.4, and the two service features above. This is the predictor set used in Section 5.

4.6 Interaction and Polynomial Terms

The features built in 4.1-4.5 enter a linear model additively, so each coefficient captures the effect of a single variable holding others constant. Transit ridership, however, is rarely additive: being car-free matters much more in a dense area than a sparse one, and the marginal effect of population density is usually larger once density is already high. To let the model capture these patterns without switching to a nonlinear method, we add a small set of two-way interactions and squared (quadratic) terms.

Interactions. Three pairs are chosen because they have clear mechanism-level interpretation:

  • no_vehicle_share × pop_density: the combination of no-vehicle households and high population density identifies the most transit-dependent neighborhoods. Neither variable alone captures this joint concentration.
  • no_vehicle_share × total_jobs: car-free residents near job-dense tracts generate commute demand specifically for transit; car-owning residents in the same tract do not.
  • n_commercial_halfmi × pop_density: commercial density and residential density together define mixed-use high-flow corridors (think Center City or University City), where ridership exceeds the sum of the two individual effects.

Polynomial (quadratic) terms. Three variables are expected to have nonlinear effects on ridership:

  • pop_density²: the relationship between density and ridership often accelerates rather than grows linearly; each additional resident per square mile adds more riders in already-dense areas.
  • no_vehicle_share²: at extreme no-vehicle shares, neighborhoods effectively rely on transit as the default travel mode, which can produce ridership levels beyond the linear trend.
  • dist_rail²: the distance-decay effect of rail proximity tends to flatten after a certain distance: being 1 mile from a rail stop versus 2 miles matters much less than being 0.1 mile versus 0.5 mile.

These six new features are added directly to stops_features. Because they combine variables on very different scales (for example, pop_density can reach tens of thousands while no_vehicle_share is bounded between 0 and 1), their raw magnitudes differ widely. This does not affect OLS coefficient validity, but we will revisit scaling in Section 5 when interpreting the coefficients and checking multicollinearity.

Code 
stops_features <- stops_features %>%
  mutate(
    no_vehicle_share = replace_na(no_vehicle_share, median(no_vehicle_share, na.rm = TRUE)),
    poverty_rate     = replace_na(poverty_rate,     median(poverty_rate,     na.rm = TRUE)),
    renter_share     = replace_na(renter_share,     median(renter_share,     na.rm = TRUE)),
    transit_share    = replace_na(transit_share,    median(transit_share,    na.rm = TRUE)),
    bachelors_share  = replace_na(bachelors_share,  median(bachelors_share,  na.rm = TRUE)),
    pop_density      = replace_na(pop_density,      median(pop_density,      na.rm = TRUE)),
    housing_density  = replace_na(housing_density,  median(housing_density,  na.rm = TRUE)),
    median_income    = replace_na(median_income,    median(median_income,    na.rm = TRUE))
  )

stops_features <- stops_features %>%
  mutate(
    # Two-way interactions
    int_novh_density       = no_vehicle_share * pop_density,
    int_novh_jobs          = no_vehicle_share * total_jobs,
    int_commercial_density = n_commercial_halfmi * pop_density,
    # Squared (quadratic) terms
    pop_density_sq         = pop_density^2,
    no_vehicle_share_sq    = no_vehicle_share^2,
    dist_rail_sq           = dist_rail^2
  )
Code 
stops_features %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(int_novh_density, int_novh_jobs, int_commercial_density,
         pop_density_sq, no_vehicle_share_sq, dist_rail_sq) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   list(Median = median, Mean = mean, Max = max),
                   .names = "{.col}__{.fn}")) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = c("Variable", "Stat"),
               names_sep = "__") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Stat, values_from = value) %>%
  mutate(across(c(Median, Mean, Max), ~ round(.x, 2))) %>%
  kable(caption = "Summary of Interaction and Polynomial Features",
        format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Summary of Interaction and Polynomial Features
Variable Median Mean Max
int_novh_density 4,378.95 5,534.33 5.086973e+04
int_novh_jobs 165.98 763.48 2.632897e+04
int_commercial_density 2,791,337.08 5,357,684.36 7.532551e+07
pop_density_sq 278,264,547.63 437,600,428.27 8.079717e+09
no_vehicle_share_sq 0.07 0.10 6.600000e-01
dist_rail_sq 3,493,895.97 17,211,880.80 2.626627e+08

With these additions, stops_features now contains the base predictors from 4.1 to 4.5 plus six engineered terms that let the model represent joint effects and nonlinear responses.

Code 
st_write(stops_features, "data/stops_features.geojson", delete_dsn = TRUE)

5. Model Building

Each modeling approach is appropriate for a different reason. The OLS models provide a useful baseline because they are easy to interpret and allow us to compare how model performance changes as additional predictors are added. We begin with demographic variables, then add distance, buffer, and service features to show which types of information improve prediction. The Poisson model is appropriate because weekday ridership is a count outcome, and Poisson regression is designed for non-negative count data. The Negative Binomial model will also be tested if ridership are strongly overdispersed, meaning the variance is much larger than the mean. In theory, Negative Binomial should be better suited to this type of uneven count data, especially when there are many low-ridership stops and a smaller number of very high-ridership stops.

5.1 Linear Regression (OLS) and Evaluation

We begin by loading the modeling-ready dataset built in Section 4. The OLS specifications below are estimated as a sequence of nested models, with each step adding a new group of predictors so that we can see which types of information actually improve fit.

Code 
# Import data
dat <- st_read("data/stops_features.geojson")

Before fitting any models, we construct one additional service-related predictor. SEPTA designates a subset of routes as frequent service routes, which run at higher headways throughout the weekday. Stops served by these routes typically attract more riders because shorter waits make transit more attractive. We count how many frequent routes serve each stop and classify the stop as Not Frequent, Frequent, or Very Frequent. This categorical predictor enters the later OLS, Poisson, and Negative Binomial models as a fixed effect.

Code 
# Adding a bus route frequency predictor

# List of septa frequent buses

freq_bus <- c(
  "3", "6", "17", "18", "21",
  "23", "33", "45", "46", "47",
  "48", "51", "52", "56", "58",
  "60", "63", "66", "70", "79",
  "82", "108", "113", "BLVDDIR"
  )

# Count frequent routes serving stops

freq_counts <- stop_ridership %>% 
  filter(route %in% freq_bus) %>% 
  group_by(stop_code) %>% 
  summarise(n_freq_routes = n_distinct(route)) %>% 
  ungroup()

dat <- dat %>% 
  left_join(freq_counts, by = "stop_code") %>% 
  mutate(
    n_freq_routes = replace_na(n_freq_routes, 0),
    frequency = case_when(
      n_freq_routes >= 2 ~ "Very Frequent",
      n_freq_routes == 1 ~ "Frequent",
      TRUE               ~ "Not Frequent"
    ),
    frequency = factor(frequency, levels = c("Not Frequent", "Frequent", "Very Frequent"))
  )

OLS 1: Demographic baseline. This first specification uses only the ACS demographic and economic predictors. It serves as a baseline to show how much weekday ridership can be explained from neighborhood demographics alone, before any spatial or service-related information is added. Comparing later models against this one tells us how much each new feature group contributes.

Show model code 
# Base OLS model with only demographic predictors from ACS data

ols1 <- lm(
  weekday_total ~
    no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
    transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs,
  data = dat
)

summary(ols1)

Call:
lm(formula = weekday_total ~ no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + 
    renter_share + transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density + 
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs, data = dat)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-478.6  -51.8  -25.2    8.8 5753.3 

Coefficients:
                   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)      -4.183e+00  1.139e+01  -0.367 0.713463    
no_vehicle_share  1.641e+02  2.157e+01   7.606 3.17e-14 ***
poverty_rate     -2.442e+01  2.369e+01  -1.031 0.302649    
renter_share     -1.727e+01  1.410e+01  -1.224 0.220877    
transit_share     5.736e+01  2.115e+01   2.711 0.006714 ** 
bachelors_share  -1.643e+02  2.759e+01  -5.956 2.70e-09 ***
pop_density       1.134e-03  4.441e-04   2.554 0.010673 *  
housing_density   5.747e-04  7.776e-04   0.739 0.459912    
median_income     4.311e-04  1.148e-04   3.754 0.000175 ***
total_jobs        9.167e-03  5.145e-04  17.818  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 166.7 on 7578 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.09687,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.0958 
F-statistic: 90.31 on 9 and 7578 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

OLS 2: Adding spatial context. This specification adds the distance-to-amenity features (school, hospital, college, park, rail) and the half-mile buffer counts (businesses, intersections, crashes, other stops, residential and vacant parcels). These features capture nearby trip generators and the built environment around each stop. We deliberately omit n_commercial_halfmi because it is highly correlated with n_businesses_halfmi, and we avoid stacking buffer counts on top of distance variables when the two clearly measure the same construct.

Show model code 
# Second OLS model with distance and buffer-based spatial predictors
# We do not include n_commercial_halfmi due to multicollinearity with n_businesses_halfmi
# We do not include buffer predictors where distance predictors are available due to multicollinearity

ols2 <- lm(
  weekday_total ~
    no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
    transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
    dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
    dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
    n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
    n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi, # end buff preds
  data = dat
)

summary(ols2)

Call:
lm(formula = weekday_total ~ no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + 
    renter_share + transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density + 
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + dist_college + 
    dist_school + dist_hospital + dist_park + dist_rail + n_businesses_halfmi + 
    n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi + n_other_stops_halfmi + 
    n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi, data = dat)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-426.6  -56.1  -23.7   15.0 5696.8 

Coefficients:
                         Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)             1.159e+02  1.724e+01   6.721 1.93e-11 ***
no_vehicle_share        7.462e+01  2.356e+01   3.168 0.001543 ** 
poverty_rate           -7.835e+01  2.488e+01  -3.149 0.001644 ** 
renter_share           -5.244e+01  1.502e+01  -3.491 0.000485 ***
transit_share           4.215e+01  2.178e+01   1.935 0.052978 .  
bachelors_share        -1.490e+02  2.896e+01  -5.146 2.72e-07 ***
pop_density             8.617e-04  5.024e-04   1.715 0.086339 .  
housing_density        -1.709e-03  8.557e-04  -1.997 0.045900 *  
median_income           5.396e-05  1.214e-04   0.444 0.656768    
total_jobs              7.320e-03  5.655e-04  12.944  < 2e-16 ***
dist_college            3.923e-04  5.469e-04   0.717 0.473180    
dist_school            -2.546e-03  2.438e-03  -1.044 0.296470    
dist_hospital          -3.059e-03  6.299e-04  -4.856 1.22e-06 ***
dist_park               2.156e-03  2.643e-03   0.816 0.414623    
dist_rail              -4.796e-03  7.831e-04  -6.124 9.58e-10 ***
n_businesses_halfmi     1.916e-02  2.169e-03   8.836  < 2e-16 ***
n_intersections_halfmi -2.476e-01  1.089e-01  -2.273 0.023040 *  
n_crashes_halfmi        1.021e-02  1.901e-02   0.537 0.591100    
n_other_stops_halfmi    3.010e-01  1.473e-01   2.043 0.041068 *  
n_residential_halfmi   -1.012e-02  2.183e-03  -4.636 3.62e-06 ***
n_vacant_halfmi        -7.272e-03  4.516e-03  -1.610 0.107376    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 164.5 on 7567 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.1225,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.1202 
F-statistic: 52.83 on 20 and 7567 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

OLS 3: Adding service features. This specification adds two stop-level service variables: n_routes, the number of bus routes serving the stop (transfer points typically attract more boardings), and has_rail_access, a binary indicator for whether a rail or trolley stop falls within the half-mile buffer. After fitting, we run vif() to flag any predictors whose variance inflation factor is too high to interpret coefficients reliably.

Show model code 
# Third OLS model with service features

ols3 <- lm(
  weekday_total ~
    no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
    transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
    dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
    dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
    n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
    n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
    n_routes + has_rail_access, # end ols3
  data = dat
)

summary(ols3)

Call:
lm(formula = weekday_total ~ no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + 
    renter_share + transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density + 
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + dist_college + 
    dist_school + dist_hospital + dist_park + dist_rail + n_businesses_halfmi + 
    n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi + n_other_stops_halfmi + 
    n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + n_routes + has_rail_access, 
    data = dat)

Residuals:

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 150.6 on 7565 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2648,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2627 
F-statistic: 123.8 on 22 and 7565 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

OLS 4: Adding fixed effects. This final OLS specification adds the service-frequency category constructed earlier and a categorical neighborhood fixed effect. Neighborhood fixed effects soak up unmeasured place-based variation (historic transit investment, local land-use patterns, walkability, perceived safety) that the ACS variables cannot fully capture. Together these two sets of fixed effects account for service quality and place identity.

Show model code 
# Third OLS model including neighborhood and service frequency fixed effects 

ols4 <- lm(
  weekday_total ~
    no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
    transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
    dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
    dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
    n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
    n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
    n_routes + has_rail_access + # end ols3
    frequency + as.factor(neighborhood), # end ols4
  data = dat
)

summary(ols4)

Call:
lm(formula = weekday_total ~ no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + 
    renter_share + transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density + 
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + dist_college + 
    dist_school + dist_hospital + dist_park + dist_rail + n_businesses_halfmi + 
    n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi + n_other_stops_halfmi + 
    n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + n_routes + has_rail_access + 
    frequency + as.factor(neighborhood), data = dat)

Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 146 on 7407 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.3233,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.3069 
F-statistic: 19.66 on 180 and 7407 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

5.2 Poisson Model and Evaluation

Because weekday_total is a non-negative count rather than a continuous outcome, a Poisson generalized linear model is a more natural specification than OLS. We use the same predictor set as OLS 4 so that the comparison is direct.

Show model code 
# Fit Poisson model

pois <- glm(
  weekday_total ~
    no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
    transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
    dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
    dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
    n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
    n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
    n_routes + has_rail_access + # end ols3
    frequency + as.factor(neighborhood), # end ols4
  data = dat,
  family = "poisson"
)

summary(pois)

Call:
glm(formula = weekday_total ~ no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + 
    renter_share + transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density + 
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + dist_college + 
    dist_school + dist_hospital + dist_park + dist_rail + n_businesses_halfmi + 
    n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi + n_other_stops_halfmi + 
    n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + n_routes + has_rail_access + 
    frequency + as.factor(neighborhood), family = "poisson", 
    data = dat)

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 1073968  on 7587  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  495454  on 7407  degrees of freedom
AIC: 535276

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 7

A key Poisson assumption is that the conditional mean equals the conditional variance (dispersion = 1). Bus ridership is unlikely to satisfy this, as a small number of high-volume corridors create much more variance than the mean alone would predict. We compute the Pearson dispersion statistic to test whether this assumption holds.

Code 
dispersion <- sum(residuals(pois, type = "pearson")^2) / pois$df.residual

cat("Dispersion parameter: ", round(dispersion, 2))
Dispersion parameter:  98.06

Since the dispersion parameter is greater than 1, we will now fit the Negative Binomial model.

5.3 Negative Binomial Model and Evaluation

Since the dispersion parameter is greater than 1, we will now fit the Negative Binomial model. We keep the same predictor set so that Poisson and Negative Binomial are directly comparable.

Show model code 
# Given extreme overdispersion, we fit a negative binomial model

nb <- glm.nb(
  weekday_total ~
    no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
    transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
    dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
    dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
    n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
    n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
    n_routes + has_rail_access + # end ols3
    frequency + as.factor(neighborhood), # end ols4
  data = dat
)

summary(nb)

Call:
glm.nb(formula = weekday_total ~ no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + 
    renter_share + transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density + 
    housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + dist_college + 
    dist_school + dist_hospital + dist_park + dist_rail + n_businesses_halfmi + 
    n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi + n_other_stops_halfmi + 
    n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + n_routes + has_rail_access + 
    frequency + as.factor(neighborhood), data = dat, init.theta = 1.163877137, 
    link = log)

Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for Negative Binomial(1.1639) family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 16850.3  on 7587  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  8678.9  on 7407  degrees of freedom
AIC: 74807

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 1

              Theta:  1.1639 
          Std. Err.:  0.0184 

 2 x log-likelihood:  -74443.4870

6. Model Comparison (LOGOCV)

Code 
neighborhood <- unique(dat$neighborhood)

all_results <- list()

# OLS1: Demographic ACS Predictors

for (i in seq_along(neighborhood)) {
  
  test_neighborhood <- neighborhood[i]
  
  # Split data
  train_data <- dat %>% filter(neighborhood != test_neighborhood)
  test_data <- dat %>% filter(neighborhood == test_neighborhood)
  
  # Fit models on training data
  
  # OLS 1: Demographic ACS Data
  ols1cv <- lm(
    weekday_total ~
      no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
      transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
      housing_density + median_income + total_jobs,
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # OLS 2: + Distance and Within Buffer Predictors
  ols2cv <- lm(
    weekday_total ~
      no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
      transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
      housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
      dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
      dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
      n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
      n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi, # end buff preds
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # OLS 3: + Service Features
  ols3cv <- lm(
    weekday_total ~
      no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
      transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
      housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
      dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
      dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
      n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
      n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
      n_routes + has_rail_access, # end ols3
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # OLS 4: + Bus Route Frequency Fixed Effects
  # Remove Neighborhood Fixed Effects for LOGOCV
  ols4cv <- lm(
    weekday_total ~
      no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
      transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
      housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
      dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
      dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
      n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
      n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
      n_routes + has_rail_access + # end ols3
      frequency, # end ols4
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # Poisson
  # Remove Neighborhood Fixed Effects for LOGOCV
  poiscv <- glm(
    weekday_total ~
      no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
      transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
      housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
      dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
      dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
      n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
      n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
      n_routes + has_rail_access + # end ols3
      frequency, # end ols4
    data = train_data,
    family = "poisson"
  )
  
  # Negative Binomial
  # Remove Neighborhood Fixed Effects for LOGOCV
  nbcv <- glm.nb(
    weekday_total ~
      no_vehicle_share + poverty_rate + renter_share +
      transit_share + bachelors_share + pop_density +
      housing_density + median_income + total_jobs + #end ols1
      dist_college + dist_school + dist_hospital + 
      dist_park + dist_rail + # end dist preds
      n_businesses_halfmi + n_intersections_halfmi + n_crashes_halfmi +
      n_other_stops_halfmi + n_residential_halfmi + n_vacant_halfmi + # end ols2
      n_routes + has_rail_access + # end ols3
      frequency, # end ols4
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # Predict on test data
  test_data <- test_data %>% 
    mutate(
      p1 = predict(ols1cv, newdata = test_data, type = "response"),
      p2 = predict(ols2cv, newdata = test_data, type = "response"),
      p3 = predict(ols3cv, newdata = test_data, type = "response"),
      p4 = predict(ols4cv, newdata = test_data, type = "response"),
      p5 = predict(poiscv, newdata = test_data, type = "response"),
      p6 = predict(nbcv, newdata = test_data, type = "response"),
    )
  
  # Calculate metrics
  fold_results <- data.frame(
    fold = test_neighborhood,
    
    mae_ols1 = mean(abs(test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p1), na.rm = TRUE),
    mae_ols2 = mean(abs(test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p2), na.rm = TRUE),
    mae_ols3 = mean(abs(test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p3), na.rm = TRUE),
    mae_ols4 = mean(abs(test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p4), na.rm = TRUE),
    mae_pois = mean(abs(test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p5), na.rm = TRUE),
    mae_nb   = mean(abs(test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p6), na.rm = TRUE),
    
    rmse_ols1 = sqrt(mean((test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p1)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
    rmse_ols2 = sqrt(mean((test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p2)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
    rmse_ols3 = sqrt(mean((test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p3)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
    rmse_ols4 = sqrt(mean((test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p4)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
    rmse_pois = sqrt(mean((test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p5)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
    rmse_nb   = sqrt(mean((test_data$weekday_total - test_data$p6)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
  )
  
  all_results[[i]] <- fold_results
}

cv_results <- bind_rows(all_results)

tibble(cv_results)
# A tibble: 157 × 13
   fold  mae_ols1 mae_ols2 mae_ols3 mae_ols4 mae_pois mae_nb rmse_ols1 rmse_ols2
   <chr>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
 1 Wiss…    286.     307.     183.     196.     222.   742.      616.      598. 
 2 Hunt…     78.2     92.7     83.2     72.6     67.5   57.9      94.3     107. 
 3 Nort…     64.5     88.1     86.8     88.7     86.3   61.0     102.      111. 
 4 Mayf…     60.2     79.5     94.3     81.6     69.9   68.6     108.      114. 
 5 Lexi…     51.5     60.3     61.1     49.9     46.3   35.3      72.2      73.8
 6 Bust…     37.1     32.2     30.8     37.2     24.3   21.4      57.6      54.3
 7 Nort…    119.      98.0    105.      81.6     34.0   32.8     123.      103. 
 8 Bybe…     62.7     62.9     54.2     49.0     25.0   22.0      70.8      69.9
 9 Some…     14.4     68.0     76.1     72.8     15.1   15.7      23.9      77.1
10 Rhaw…     45.6     49.8     43.3     43.7     49.5   38.9     112.      107. 
# ℹ 147 more rows
# ℹ 4 more variables: rmse_ols3 <dbl>, rmse_ols4 <dbl>, rmse_pois <dbl>,
#   rmse_nb <dbl>
Code 
cv_summary_combined <- cv_results %>%
  summarize(
    across(
      where(is.numeric),
      list(mean = mean, median = median),
      na.rm = TRUE
    )
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = c("metric", "model", "stat"),
    names_pattern = "(mae|rmse)_(.*)_(mean|median)"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    model = case_when(
      model == "ols1" ~ "OLS 1: Base Demographic ACS Data",
      model == "ols2" ~ "OLS 2: + Distance and Buffer Predictors",
      model == "ols3" ~ "OLS 3: + Service Features",
      model == "ols4" ~ "OLS 4: + Frequency FE",
      model == "pois" ~ "Poisson",
      model == "nb"   ~ "Negative Binomial"
    ),
    metric = toupper(metric),
    stat = str_to_title(stat)
  ) %>%
  unite("column", stat, metric, sep = " ") %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = column,
    values_from = value
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    "Model" = model,
    "Mean MAE",
    "Median MAE",
    "Mean RMSE",
    "Median RMSE"
  )

cv_summary_combined %>%
  kable(digits = 2) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"))
Model Mean MAE Median MAE Mean RMSE Median RMSE
OLS 1: Base Demographic ACS Data 65.80 53.51 108.32 75.15
OLS 2: + Distance and Buffer Predictors 67.09 56.35 108.05 75.85
OLS 3: + Service Features 64.85 53.63 102.75 74.65
OLS 4: + Frequency FE 62.15 48.96 100.22 73.30
Poisson 55.27 44.60 96.59 63.38
Negative Binomial 78.44 43.80 199.10 64.95

Notes about RMSE Averages:

The Negative Binomial model is extremely sensitive to leaving out several high-ridership neighborhoods when compared to Poisson which is displayed by its extreme prediction errors in these cases:

Code 
cv_results %>%
  dplyr::select(fold, rmse_nb, rmse_pois) %>%
  arrange(desc(rmse_nb)) %>%
  DT::datatable(
    options = list(
      pageLength = 8,
      scrollY = "300px",
      scrollCollapse = TRUE
    )
  )

Overall, the Negative Binomial model is selected as the final model for this analysis because weekday bus stop ridership is an overdispersed count outcome. The dependent variable is a count of riders, so a count model is more appropriate than a linear regression model. The OLS models were useful as baseline comparisons because they showed how prediction changed as different variables were added. However, OLS is still a linear model and is not designed for count outcomes. It can produce predictions that do not match the structure of ridership data, especially when the outcome is highly uneven across stops.

The Poisson model was also tested because it is designed for count data. In cross-validation, it produced the lowest average prediction errors among the models tested, which suggests that treating ridership as a count outcome improved predictive performance. However, the Poisson model had a very high dispersion parameter. This is a major limitation because a standard Poisson model assumes that the conditional mean and variance of the outcome are approximately equal. Bus stop ridership does not meet this assumption. Most stops have relatively low weekday ridership, while a smaller number of stops in major corridors, transfer areas, or high-activity neighborhoods have very high ridership. This creates overdispersion, meaning the variance is much larger than the mean.

For this reason, the Poisson model is not selected as the final model even though it performed well on MAE and RMSE. The Negative Binomial model is preferred because it directly accounts for extra variation in count data beyond what the Poisson model allows. This makes it a more statistically appropriate model for explaining and diagnosing SEPTA bus stop ridership patterns.

6.1 Error Diagnostics

Because the Negative Binomial model had the lowest cross-validated MAE and RMSE, we use it as the final predictive model for the spatial error diagnostics. Mapping its prediction errors helps evaluate whether the model performs evenly across Philadelphia or whether it systematically underpredicts or overpredicts ridership in certain areas.

Code 
# Create stop-level prediction errors
dat_errors <- dat %>%
  mutate(
    pred_nb = predict(nb, newdata = dat, type = "response"),
    error = weekday_total - pred_nb
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(
    data = philly,
    fill = "grey95",
    color = "grey60",
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_sf(
    data = dat_errors,
    aes(color = error),
    size = 0.7,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_color_gradient2(
    low = "#2C7BB6",
    mid = "white",
    high = "#D7191C",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits = c(-100, 100),
    oob = scales::squish,
    name = "Error\nActual - Predicted",
    breaks = c(-100, -50, 0, 50, 100),
    labels = c("-100+", "-50", "0", "50", "100+")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Bus Stop-Level Prediction Errors",
    subtitle = "Red = underpredicted, blue = overpredicted"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14)
  )

This map shows prediction errors at the individual bus stop level by comparing actual weekday ridership to the model’s predicted ridership. Red points represent stops where actual ridership was higher than predicted, meaning the model underpredicted demand. Blue points represent stops where actual ridership was lower than predicted, meaning the model overpredicted demand. Lighter points indicate smaller errors, while darker points indicate larger errors. Overall, the map shows that prediction errors are not evenly distributed across Philadelphia. Many stops have relatively small errors, but there are visible clusters of stronger red and blue errors along major bus corridors and in higher-activity areas. This suggests that the model captures the general spatial pattern of ridership, but it still struggles at specific stops where local demand conditions are more unusual, such as transfer points, dense commercial areas, or corridors with especially high service activity.

7. Conclusion and Implementation

This report developed a predictive model for estimating average weekday ridership at SEPTA bus stops in Philadelphia. The goal was to support transit planning decisions by identifying what types of locations are likely to generate stronger ridership demand. Because bus stop ridership is shaped by neighborhood demographics, nearby destinations, land use, transit access, and service characteristics, the analysis combined ACS demographic variables, spatial proximity measures, buffer-based built environment variables, and service features such as route count and frequency category.

Several limitations should be considered when interpreting these results. First, the model is based on existing stop locations, so it learns from places where SEPTA already provides service. A proposed new stop may differ from existing stops in ways the model cannot fully capture. Second, some important factors are not included, such as sidewalk quality, stop amenities, bus reliability, schedule frequency by time of day, rider demographics at finer scales, safety perceptions, and special event demand. Finally, the cross-validation approach left out neighborhood fixed effects to avoid prediction failure when testing on unseen neighborhoods, so the comparison reflects a more generalizable version of the model rather than the full fitted specification.

For implementation, this model should be used as a decision-support tool rather than a final decision rule. SEPTA or the City of Philadelphia could apply the model to candidate bus stop locations to estimate likely weekday ridership and compare alternatives. Locations with higher predicted ridership could be prioritized for further review, especially when they also improve access for transit-dependent populations or connect to major destinations. However, a key risk is that the model may favor places that already resemble existing high-ridership stops, while overlooking neighborhoods with high transit need but weaker existing service. Because the model is trained on current ridership patterns, it may also reflect past inequities in transit access. If some neighborhoods have historically received less frequent service, fewer nearby stops, or weaker connections to major destinations, the model could learn those patterns and unintentionally continue to under-prioritize them. For this reason, model results should be interpreted alongside equity, safety, and community-based criteria. In this sense, the model is best understood as an initial screening tool that can help planners identify promising locations, while still requiring local knowledge and equity review evaluation before any service changes are made.

Note: Portions of the written text in this report were assisted by ChatGPT for grammatical correction, stylistic refinement, and clarity of expression. ChatGPT was also used to help identify coding errors and improve code readability. All analytical decisions, model construction, statistical interpretation, and final conclusions were determined and approved by the author.

Source Code
---
title: "Predicting SEPTA Bus Stop Ridership"
subtitle: "CPLN 5920 - Spring 2026"
author: "Coco Zhou, Parker Otto, Veronica Zheng, Zhuosi Yang"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# 1. Introduction

Philadelphia's bus network plays an important role in connecting residents to jobs, schools, services, and other daily destinations. When the city or SEPTA considers adding a new bus stop, one important planning question is whether that location is likely to attract enough riders to justify the investment. Because ridership is shaped by more than the stop itself, this question requires looking at the surrounding neighborhood context, including who lives nearby, how people travel, what land uses are present, and how the proposed stop fits into the existing transit network.

This report develops a predictive model to estimate expected average daily ridership at potential bus stop locations in Philadelphia. The model uses demographic, land-use, and spatial characteristics around existing stops to understand which factors are associated with higher or lower weekday ridership. By learning from current stop patterns, the model can be used as a planning tool to compare candidate locations for future bus stops.

**Data Sources**: This analysis uses four types of data. The main dataset is the [SEPTA Summer 2025 Bus Stop Summary](https://opendataphilly.org/datasets/septa-ridership-statistics/) from OpenDataPhilly, which gives average daily boardings and alightings at every bus stop collected by Automatic Passenger Counters. Demographic features come from the [American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 5-Year Estimates](https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/acs) at the Census tract level, accessed with the `tidycensus` R package. These include no-vehicle household share, poverty rate, population density, and transit commute share. Employment data come from the [LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics (LODES)](https://lehd.ces.census.gov/data/) dataset produced by the U.S. Census Bureau. Total job counts are aggregated to the Census tract level and joined to bus stops to measure nearby employment activity. Spatial features come from several [OpenDataPhilly](https://opendataphilly.org/) datasets, including schools, hospitals, colleges, crash locations, business licenses, parcels, parks, and neighborhood boundaries. The city boundary is pulled with the `tigris` R package.

# 2. Data Preparation & Cleaning

```{r setup}

library(MASS)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(tidycensus)
library(tigris)
library(janitor)
library(lubridate)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(gridExtra)
library(scales)
library(car)

httr::set_config(httr::config(ssl_verifypeer = FALSE))
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)
```

## 2.1 Read Ridership Data

The primary dataset is the **SEPTA Summer 2025 Bus Stop Summary**, which provides average daily boardings and alightings at every bus stop, broken down by weekday, Saturday, and Sunday. Data are collected via Automatic Passenger Counters (APC).

> **Download:** If you do not have the dataset, download it from [SEPTA Ridership Statistics on OpenDataPhilly](https://opendataphilly.org/datasets/septa-ridership-statistics/). Select the Bus Stop Summary file and save it to your local `data/` folder as `Summer_2025_Stop_Summary_(Bus).csv`.

```{r read-ridership}

stop_ridership <- read_csv("data/Summer_2025_Stop_Summary_(Bus).csv") %>%
  clean_names()
```

```{r explore-ridership}

head(stop_ridership, 10)
cat("Total stop-route observations:", nrow(stop_ridership), "\n")
cat("Unique stop codes:", n_distinct(stop_ridership$stop_code), "\n")
cat("Unique routes:", n_distinct(stop_ridership$route), "\n")
```

```{r aggregate-ridership}

# Aggregate to one row per unique stop
# Some stops appear on multiple routes, so we sum ridership across routes per stop
stop_ridership_agg <- stop_ridership %>%
  group_by(stop_code, stop, lat, lon, x, y) %>%
  summarise(
    weekday_boardings   = sum(weekdays_o, na.rm = TRUE),
    weekday_alightings  = sum(weekdays_1, na.rm = TRUE),
    saturday_boardings  = sum(saturdays,  na.rm = TRUE),
    saturday_alightings = sum(saturdays1, na.rm = TRUE),
    sunday_boardings    = sum(sundays_on, na.rm = TRUE),
    sunday_alightings   = sum(sundays_of, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_routes            = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    weekday_total  = weekday_boardings  + weekday_alightings,
    saturday_total = saturday_boardings + saturday_alightings,
    sunday_total   = sunday_boardings   + sunday_alightings
  )
```

Because the raw ridership file is organized at the route-stop level, some physical stops appear more than once when they are served by multiple routes or directions. To create a stop-level ridership measure, we grouped the data by stop code, stop name, and coordinates, then summed boardings and alightings across route records for each stop. This produces one total weekday ridership value for each unique stop.

```{r}
stop_ridership_agg %>%
  summarise(
    count = n(),
    min_weekday = min(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_weekday = median(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_weekday = mean(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_weekday = max(weekday_total, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

```

From the table we can see that a few stops are very busy while many stops are not. This shows that our data is very uneven.

After aggregating to one row per unique stop, the dataset contains **`r nrow(stop_ridership_agg)` stops**. SEPTA records ridership for three day types: weekday, Saturday, and Sunday. The chart below compares average total activity per stop across all three day types.

```{r day-comparison}
#| fig-cap: "Average Total Activity per Stop by Day Type"
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

stop_ridership_agg %>%
  summarise(
    Weekday  = mean(weekday_total,  na.rm = TRUE),
    Saturday = mean(saturday_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Sunday   = mean(sunday_total,   na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Day Type", values_to = "Avg Total") %>%
  mutate(`Day Type` = factor(`Day Type`, levels = c("Weekday", "Saturday", "Sunday"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = `Day Type`, y = `Avg Total`, fill = `Day Type`)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2C7BB6", "#ABD9E9", "#FDAE61")) +
  labs(title = "Average Total Activity per Stop by Day Type",
       x = NULL, y = "Avg Boardings + Alightings") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

Weekday activity is much higher than Saturday and Sunday. Therefore, we will be using **weekday total** (boardings + alightings) as the outcome variable because it captures the most complete and policy-relevant demand signal for a typical workday.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2.2 Census / ACS Data

ACS 2023 5-year estimates are pulled at the Census tract level for Philadelphia County. These variables capture the demographic and socioeconomic characteristics most linked to transit demand.

> **API Key:** A free Census API key is required. Register at [api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html](https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html) and set it once with `census_api_key("YOUR_KEY_HERE", install = TRUE)`.

The following features were selected from the ACS:

- **Total population** (`B01003_001`). Baseline count of how many people live near a stop.
- **Median household income** (`B19013_001`). Income shapes car ownership and how much people depend on transit.
- **No-vehicle households** (`B08201_002` / `B08201_001`). Computed as a share. Car-free households are the most transit-dependent group.
- **Poverty rate** (`B17001_002` / `B17001_001`). Low-income residents are more likely to use transit for daily trips.
- **Renter share** (`B25003_003` / `B25003_001`). Renters tend to live in denser areas and rely on transit more.
- **Transit commute share** (`B08301_010` / `B08301_001`). The share of workers who already commute by public transit, which is a direct signal of existing demand.
